html:
A Christmas Carol — 10 Most Likely AQA Questions + Skeleton Essay Plans

For: AQA GCSE English Literature, Paper 1 Section B (19th-century novel). 30 marks. ~50 minutes.

Source. The plans below distil the ideas, frames and analytical moves from Sophicly's 9 Grade-9 A Christmas Carol model answers (`Model Answers/AQA/A Christmas Carol/`). Each plan reuses the same load-bearing scenes, the same controlling concept, and the same reading-strategies students have already learned in the A Christmas Carol crib sheet.

How to use this document. Each entry below gives:

1. The AQA-style question.

2. The full typical extract in a blockquote so the student knows what scene to expect.

3. The frame line (the controlling argument behind the plan).

4. A short technique-term gloss block so every term in the plan is translated into plain English.

5. A skeleton plan in the locked structure used across every Sophicly worked essay:

• Introduction — 3 sentences (~110 words): Hook + Building sentence + Thesis.

• Body Paragraph 1 — 7 sentences (Beginning of the novella; ~210–225 words).

• Body Paragraph 2 — 7 sentences (Middle of the novella; the printed extract usually sits here; ~225–250 words).

• Body Paragraph 3 — 7 sentences (End of the novella; ~225–250 words).

• Conclusion — 4 sentences (~110–155 words).

Total ~1000 words on the page in the exam.

The seven-sentence body-paragraph shape (memorise this):

1. Topic — pure concept, no techniques named yet.

2. Technique + evidence — name the technique and quote the line.

3. Close analysis — the words inside the quote, unpacked.

4. Effect on reader 1 — what the reader feels or thinks.

5. Effect on reader 2 — a different feeling or thought.

6. Author's purpose — what Dickens may be arguing.

7. Context (AO3) — one factor, woven in, never bolted on.

Each plan reuses the same load-bearing scenes — the A Christmas Carol corpus rests on Stave 1 (counting-house, Fred's visit, charity gentlemen, Marley's chains), Stave 2 (boarding school, Fan, Fezziwig, Belle), Stave 3 (Cratchit dinner, Tiny Tim, Ignorance and Want), Stave 4 (Old Joe, the empty stool, the gravestone), and Stave 5 (the awakening, the turkey, Bob's raised salary, "second father" to Tim). Memorise these scenes once, with two embedded quotations per scene, and every AQA question becomes a recombination of the same corpus.

---

Memorise these once

Controlling concept of A Christmas Carol. Money without mercy is a form of living death; charity made active is rebirth. The wealthy are forged into chains by their own ledgers, but those chains can still be unlocked — until the moment of the gravestone, after which they cannot.

Six key terms that recur in the plans below — gloss every one when you use it.

• Novella = a short novel, longer than a short story but shorter than a full novel; A Christmas Carol is paced for a single sitting on Christmas Eve.

• Stave = a verse of music, like a hymn; Dickens names his five chapters "Staves" to lean on the Carol form, framing the novella as a moral hymn delivered in five movements.

• Allegory = a narrative in which characters or images stand for abstract ideas; Ignorance and Want in Stave 3 are allegorical figures, not real children.

• Anagnorisis = the moment of recognition in a tragic arc; Scrooge's anagnorisis arrives at his own gravestone in Stave 4.

• Cash-nexus = Thomas Carlyle's 1843 term for a society where money has replaced every other moral relationship between human beings; Marley's chain is the cash-nexus made into iron.

• Free indirect discourse = a narrative voice that slides between the narrator and the character's interior, so the reader hears what the character feels even while the narrator judges; Dickens uses it to merge with Scrooge in Stave 1 and dissolve the merge by Stave 5.

Always gloss the term in brackets the first time you use it in an essay. AQA examiners reward the term + the gloss together.

---

Q1 — Scrooge as a character

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Dickens presents the character of Scrooge in A Christmas Carol.

>

Write about:

>

- how Dickens presents Scrooge in this extract

- how Dickens presents Scrooge in the novel as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Asyndetic verb-catalogue = a list of verbs run together without conjunctions, so the rhythm imitates the relentlessness of the action being listed.

• Caricature = exaggerated portrait that selects one or two traits and makes them visible; Dickens's signature mode.

• Simile = a direct comparison using "like" or "as" ("solitary as an oyster").

• Apostrophe = direct narrator-address to the reader or to a character.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Parallelism with repetition = matching grammatical patterns ringing the same word ("as good a friend, as good a master, as good a man") so the word becomes the verdict.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Dickens composed A Christmas Carol in only six weeks during the autumn of 1843, immediately after his October address at the Manchester Athenaeum on the suffering of working-class children.

• Building sentence (historical context): That moment sat at the height of the Hungry Forties, when the 1834 New Poor Law had condemned paupers to workhouses on the principle of "less eligibility". In the same year, Thomas Carlyle's Past and Present (April 1843) had diagnosed Britain as a society where the only remaining bond between rich and poor was the cash-nexus — the ledger-hardened soul Dickens's novella exists to attack.

• Thesis: Across the narrator's Stave-1 verdict on Scrooge as a closed "covetous old sinner", the Stave-4 gravestone where Scrooge finally reads his own ending, and the Stave-5 parallelism that names him "as good a friend, as good a master, as good a man", Dickens presents Scrooge as a man whose hardened self can still be unforged provided the recognition arrives before the grave.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Stave 1, 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 1, the counting-house — narrator's opening verdict)

• Topic: In the opening Stave's counting-house portrait, Dickens installs Scrooge as a closed self whose hardened character is the moral residue of a life lived inside the cash-nexus.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "a squeezing, wrenching, grasping, scraping, clutching, covetous old sinner!" combines an asyndetic verb-catalogue (five present-participle verbs piled without conjunctions) with the simile "solitary as an oyster" (Scrooge's selfhood compared to a sealed shell from which no warmth escapes).

• Close analysis: The five verbs all describe acquisitive hand-actions, escalating from "squeezing" into the harsher "clutching", and the noun "sinner" delivers a religious verdict the reader cannot unhear.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears the rhythm of relentless grasping and feels Scrooge's interior as a hungry, closed appetite.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader registers the simile of the oyster as a moral diagnosis: a life sealed against everything outside it.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be reaching for the medieval morality-play tradition of Everyman (where character names the moral state) so that Scrooge's hidden moral condition becomes legible on the surface of the prose.

• Context (AO3): Carlyle's Past and Present, published earlier that same year, had named the cash-nexus precisely; the asyndetic verb-catalogue is the cash-nexus turned into rhythm.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Stave 4, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words; the printed extract is from Stave 1, but the central recognition lives in Stave 4)

Scene focus: (Stave 4, the gravestone — anagnorisis)

• Topic: As the novella reaches its turning point, Dickens dramatises Scrooge's character cracking open at the moment he finally reads his own ending.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "his own name, EBENEZER SCROOGE… Am I that man who lay upon the bed?" fuses the typographical capitalisation (the name on the stone printed in small capitals so the page reads like a gravestone) with the rhetorical question (Scrooge interrogating his own identity in the third person inside the same recognition-moment).

• Close analysis: The capitalised letters collapse the distance between Scrooge's eyes and the reader's, and the third-person "that man" dramatises the protagonist split from himself at the very stone that names him.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader watches the recognition (the anagnorisis — the moment a tragic figure understands what has been happening) dramatised on the page itself.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader experiences the gravestone as the latest possible moment for repentance and registers Scrooge's terror as the engine of the change to come.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be compressing the entire arc of a tragic protagonist into a single image — a closed self breaking open against the fact of its own death — so that the wealthy Victorian reader is invited to perform the same recognition without needing the same vision.

• Context (AO3): Within Christian conversion-narrative tradition (The Pilgrim's Progress, 1678), recognition followed by repentance is the necessary pivot before redemption; Dickens secularises that tradition into a moral economics for a capital city.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Stave 5, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 5, the closing verdict — Scrooge reborn)

• Topic: As the novella closes, Dickens turns the narrator's opening verdict inside out — the "covetous old sinner" becomes a fatherly benefactor whose visible generosity is the opposite of greed.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "as good a friend, as good a master, and as good a man, as the good old city knew" combines parallelism (matching grammatical patterns) with heavy repetition (the word "good" ringing four times in seventeen words).

• Close analysis: The repeated phrase "as good as" cycles four times until the word "good" stops needing a modifier and simply becomes Scrooge's new identity.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears the repeated word "good" as moral healing, the verdict of the opening Stave finally reversed in the ear.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader recognises Scrooge has accepted the public roles — friend, master, man — that the closed Stave-1 self had refused.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be issuing a choice to his middle-class readers — be Marley (the bad capitalist whose chain of ledgers shackles him after death) or be Fezziwig and the post-redemption Scrooge (the good capitalists whose wealth becomes warmth).

• Context (AO3): The novella appeared at exactly the moment Dickens and his contemporaries were re-inventing the modern English Christmas — Prince Albert's tree (1841), Henry Cole's first commercial Christmas card (1843) and Dickens's own near-singular role in turning the festival from an unevenly observed feast into a national moral ritual.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through the narrator's Stave-1 verdict, Scrooge's Stave-4 anagnorisis at his own gravestone, and the Stave-5 parallelism that renames him "as good a friend, as good a master, as good a man", Dickens presents Scrooge as a closed self whose hardened character can still be unforged provided the recognition arrives before the gravestone closes the door.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that money without mercy is a form of living death, and that charity made active is rebirth.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be issuing a choice to his middle-class readers — be Marley, whose ledgers shackle his ghost, or be the post-redemption Scrooge, whose wealth becomes warmth.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that character is not destiny, that the door from the counting-house to Tiny Tim's hearth stays open until the gravestone closes it, and that the cure for the hardened self is recognition followed by action.

Q2 — Marley and the theme of guilt

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Dickens presents the theme of guilt in A Christmas Carol.

>

Write about:

>

- how Dickens presents guilt in this extract

- how Dickens presents guilt in the novel as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Symbolism = an object made to stand for an abstract idea (Marley's chain as guilt made physical).

• Metonymy = a thing named for something it is closely associated with — "ledgers" standing in for the entire counting-house life that produced them.

• Polysyndeton = many conjunctions strung together ("link by link, and yard by yard") so the rhythm imitates the slow forging of the chain.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Caricature = a moral portrait drawn in heightened lines.

• Foreshadowing = early hints of later events.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): In September 1843, three months before A Christmas Carol appeared, Dickens visited the Field Lane Ragged School in Saffron Hill — a charitable school for the destitute children of the rookeries — and wrote afterwards of having seen children sleeping rough in coal-cellars within walking distance of the City counting-houses.

• Building sentence (historical context): That visit, followed by his Manchester Athenaeum address on 5 October 1843, sat against the wider economic background of the Hungry Forties. The 1834 New Poor Law had institutionalised pauper-suffering inside the workhouse, and Carlyle's Past and Present (April 1843) had named the cash-nexus that bound rich to poor through ledgers alone.

• Thesis: Across Marley's chained warning in Stave 1, the Old Joe scene in Stave 4 where Scrooge's stolen possessions become a posthumous indictment, and the Stave-5 awakening that breaks the unforged chain, Dickens presents guilt as a debt the wealthy dead pay for the suffering they refused to relieve in life.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Stave 1, the extract — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 1, Marley's chained visitation)

• Topic: In Marley's first speech, Dickens makes guilt visible as a chain forged by the wearer in life and worn helplessly after death.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I wear the chain I forged in life. I made it link by link, and yard by yard" combines symbolism (the chain as guilt made physical) with polysyndeton (the conjunctions "and" binding "link by link, and yard by yard" so the rhythm imitates the slow accumulation).

• Close analysis: The verb "forged" names guilt as an action the agent performs on himself, and the polysyndetic rhythm dramatises that this forging happened slowly, daily, choice by choice.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears the rhythm of accumulation and registers guilt not as a single sin but as the patient work of a life.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader watches the chain become visible to Scrooge and feels the warning land — what is invisible inside the wearer in life becomes visible outside the wearer in death.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be drawing on the medieval morality-play tradition (Everyman, The Pilgrim's Progress) where moral states are made visible on the body, in order to give the wealthy Victorian reader a portrait of his or her own future self.

• Context (AO3): Carlyle's Past and Present (April 1843) had argued that the cash-nexus society was forging chains the rich could not see; Marley's chain is that diagnosis turned into iron.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Stave 4, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 4, the Old Joe scene — guilt without mourning)

• Topic: In the rag-and-bone shop of Old Joe, Dickens depicts guilt as the consequence of a life that left no one to mourn it.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): The laundress's verdict "He frightened every one away from him when he was alive, to profit us when he was dead" combines antithesis ("alive" against "dead"; "frightened away" against "profit") with grim irony (the man who refused human company in life is now stripped naked by strangers in death).

• Close analysis: The antithesis pivots on the preposition "to" — the dead man's whole utility is now "to profit" the people who are dismantling his goods.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader pities a corpse pictured as unattended on a stripped bed and shudders at the laundress's matter-of-fact tone.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader recognises the scene as the practical proof of Marley's warning — guilt accumulated in life produces a death without mourners.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be dramatising the social consequence of the cash-nexus — the man who treated his fellow human beings as ledger-entries is treated as one ledger-entry himself once the breath has left him.

• Context (AO3): The 1834 New Poor Law had made pauper deaths similarly impersonal — paupers buried in unmarked workhouse graves; Dickens reverses the social position but keeps the fate, so the unattended corpse on the stripped bed is the wealthy reader's own possible ending.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Stave 5, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 5, the awakening — unforging the chain)

• Topic: In the morning that follows the recognition, Dickens portrays guilt unforged before it accumulates the weight Marley's chain had carried.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "They are not torn down, they are not torn down, rings and all. They are here — I am here — the shadows of the things that would have been, may be dispelled" combines anaphora (the repeated "they are") with the dash-driven dashes that visually fracture Scrooge's speech as joy breaks the closed sentences open.

• Close analysis: The dashes split Scrooge's speech into fragments and the repeated "they are" ties present-tense reality to a future Scrooge can still rewrite — the verb-tense pivot from "would have been" to "may be dispelled" dramatises guilt's reversal in real time.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears the broken, joyful syntax and feels the relief of a man whose chain has not yet hardened.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader recognises that what is "dispelled" is not the past but the future — the Stave-4 gravestone has been refused by the Stave-5 self.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be writing the door of repentance open for the wealthy reader — guilt is not yet fixed; the chain is still being forged; the hammer is still in the wearer's own hand.

• Context (AO3): Within the Christian repentance tradition Dickens inherited from Bunyan, the moment of recognition followed by action is the necessary pivot; Dickens secularises that tradition for the counting-house economy of his own century.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through Marley's chained Stave-1 warning, the Old Joe rag-and-bone shop in Stave 4, and the dash-driven Stave-5 awakening, Dickens presents guilt as a chain forged in life — a debt the wealthy dead pay for the suffering they refused to relieve, but a chain that can still be unforged while the wearer is alive.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that money without mercy is a form of living death, and that the chain of guilt is the cash-nexus made into iron.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be issuing a moral warning to his middle-class readers — the chain is being forged daily, choice by choice, and only present recognition can prevent its posthumous weight.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that guilt is not a single sin but a slow accumulation, that the wealthy dead are weighed by what they refused to see in life, and that the door from Marley's chain to Scrooge's morning stays open until the gravestone closes it.

Q3 — The theme of redemption

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Dickens presents the theme of redemption in A Christmas Carol.

>

Write about:

>

- how Dickens presents redemption in this extract

- how Dickens presents redemption in the novel as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Asyndetic verb-catalogue = list of verbs without conjunctions, the rhythm imitating relentless action.

• Tricolon / triadic listing = three items in a row, often building an emotional climax.

• Hyperbole = deliberate exaggeration ("as light as a feather, as happy as an angel").

• Simile = direct comparison using "like" or "as".

• Apostrophe = direct address by the speaker to someone absent or to the city itself.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): On 5 October 1843, six weeks before he began writing A Christmas Carol, Dickens addressed the Manchester Athenaeum on the moral and economic case for educating poor children.

• Building sentence (historical context): That moment sat inside a decade in which the Hungry Forties had visibly hardened London — the 1834 New Poor Law had institutionalised pauper-suffering, and the Irish Famine was a year away. Carlyle's Past and Present (April 1843) had argued that Britain's wealthy classes had been spiritually cauterised by the cash-nexus and required not new policy but moral conversion.

• Thesis: Across the narrator's Stave-1 verdict on the closed "covetous old sinner", the Stave-4 gravestone where Scrooge finally recognises his own ending, and the Stave-5 awakening where joy breaks the hardened self into broken, jubilant speech, Dickens stages redemption as the conversion of inward recognition into visible action — a door that stays open until the gravestone closes it.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Stave 1, 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 1, the closed self)

• Topic: In the opening Stave's counting-house verdict, Dickens installs the hardened self that the rest of the novella will exist to redeem.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "a squeezing, wrenching, grasping, scraping, clutching, covetous old sinner!" combines an asyndetic verb-catalogue (five present-participle verbs piled without conjunctions) with caricature (the closing noun "sinner" fixing the moral verdict on the surface of the prose).

• Close analysis: The five verbs all describe the same hand-action of seizing, intensifying from "squeezing" to "clutching", and the noun "sinner" delivers a religious indictment the reader cannot unhear.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader feels the rhythm of relentless grasping and registers Scrooge's interior as a closed, hungry appetite.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader recognises the moral diagnosis the narrator is making — Scrooge is not unwell, he is guilty; the diagnosis is theological before it is psychological.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be installing the maximum-distance Scrooge so the eventual redemption travels the maximum moral arc; the closer the Stave-1 self stands to damnation, the further the Stave-5 self travels.

• Context (AO3): The morality-play tradition (Everyman, c. 1500) staged moral states as visible on the body so the audience could read them; Dickens is reaching back through that tradition for a Victorian readership that increasingly preferred its morals legible.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Stave 4, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 4, the gravestone — the point of recognition)

• Topic: At the novella's turning point, Dickens stages redemption made possible only because Scrooge has finally read his own ending.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "his own name, EBENEZER SCROOGE" and "Am I that man who lay upon the bed?" combine the rhetorical question (Scrooge interrogating his own identity in the third person) with typographical capitalisation (the name on the stone printed in small capitals so the page reads like a gravestone).

• Close analysis: The third-person "that man" dramatises the protagonist split from himself, and the capitalised letters on the page collapse the distance between Scrooge's eyes and the reader's.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader watches the recognition (the anagnorisis — the moment a tragic figure understands what has been happening) dramatised on the page itself.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader experiences the gravestone as the latest possible moment for repentance and feels the door of redemption still — only just — ajar.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be staging recognition as the necessary pivot before action; redemption that begins anywhere but at one's own gravestone, the structure of the novella implies, will not hold.

• Context (AO3): Within Christian conversion-narrative tradition (Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress, 1678), recognition followed by repentance is the necessary pivot before redemption; Dickens secularises that tradition into the moral economics of a counting-house century.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Stave 5, the extract — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 5, the awakening — visible rebirth)

• Topic: In the morning after the gravestone, Dickens presents redemption converted from inward recognition into joyful, visible action.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I am as light as a feather, I am as happy as an angel, I am as merry as a schoolboy" combines a tricolon (three balanced clauses, each opening "I am as…") with hyperbole (the climbing similes — feather, angel, schoolboy — escalating from physical to spiritual to childlike joy).

• Close analysis: Each "I am as…" clause names a different dimension of the new self; the noun "schoolboy" returns Scrooge to the lonely boarding-school child of Stave 2, redeeming the loneliness backwards.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader catches the climbing rhythm of the tricolon and feels the lift of a man whose burdens have been suspended in a single morning.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader watches the broken syntax — Scrooge laughing and crying "in the same breath" — and registers redemption as something embodied, not announced.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be dramatising redemption as visible joy rather than private piety — a public performance of the changed self, addressed to the city, witnessed by anyone who passes.

• Context (AO3): The novella appeared at exactly the moment Dickens and his contemporaries were re-inventing the modern English Christmas — Prince Albert's tree (1841), Henry Cole's first commercial Christmas card (1843) and Dickens's own near-singular role in turning the festival into a national moral ritual; Stave 5's joy is also the festival's joy, the two arriving together.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through the Stave-1 narrator's verdict on the "covetous old sinner", the Stave-4 gravestone where Scrooge finally reads his own ending, and the Stave-5 awakening that converts recognition into visible joy, Dickens presents redemption as a three-part rhythm — diagnosis, recognition, action — kept open until the gravestone closes it.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that money without mercy is a form of living death and that charity made active is rebirth.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be writing the door of repentance open for his middle-class readers — none of them too closed to be unforged, none of them too late so long as the gravestone has not yet been read.

• Ultimate moral: The final message, given to the smallest character in the smallest sentence — "God bless Us, Every One!" — is that redemption is not solitary but communal, and that the test of a redeemed self is whether "Us, Every One" has become its first thought.

Q4 — The importance of family

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Dickens presents the importance of family in A Christmas Carol.

>

Write about:

>

- how Dickens presents family in this extract

- how Dickens presents family in the novel as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Polysyndeton = many conjunctions strung together ("and" repeated), making the rhythm cumulative and inclusive.

• Tricolon / triadic listing = three items in a row.

• Anaphora = repetition at the start of clauses.

• Hyperbole = deliberate exaggeration.

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Fred and Bob both function as foils to Scrooge).

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The 1834 Poor Law Amendment Act had made the workhouse the principal English instrument for managing pauperism — and one of its central provisions was the segregation of families on entry, husband from wife, parent from child, sleeping in separate dormitories so that pauper households were broken up at the threshold.

• Building sentence (historical context): Inside that institutional context, working-class families like the Cratchits — fifteen-shillings-a-week clerks paid below the cost of feeding a household — lived one illness or one master's-firing away from the workhouse door. The family table was the last private space before the public dormitory.

• Thesis: Across Scrooge's Stave-1 dismissal of Fred and underpayment of Bob, the Stave-3 Cratchit dinner where collective joy operates inside material poverty, and the Stave-5 entry into Fred's house where the closed self finally crosses a family threshold, Dickens presents family as the moral counterweight to the cash-nexus — the human relationship money cannot price and the workhouse cannot legislate away.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Stave 1, 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 1, Fred dismissed — the closed self refusing family)

• Topic: In the opening Stave, Dickens illustrates family refused — Scrooge dismissing Fred's invitation and Bob's family by paying him below the cost of feeding them.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Bah! Humbug!" combines the monosyllabic exclamation (a two-word verdict that closes off conversation) with onomatopoeia (the harsh "Bah!" sounding the dismissal it performs).

• Close analysis: The two-word reply is grammatically complete, leaves no opening for reply, and is repeated through the Stave so the rhythm of dismissal becomes Scrooge's signature speech-act.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader registers the monosyllabic refusal and registers it as the auditory portrait of the closed self.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader registers Fred — a foil to Scrooge — as the family-threshold Scrooge will not cross until Stave 5.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be staging the closed self at maximum distance from family so that the Stave-5 reconciliation can travel the maximum moral arc.

• Context (AO3): Carlyle's Past and Present (April 1843) had diagnosed the period's wealthy as men who had reduced human relationships to ledger-entries; "Bah! Humbug!" is that diagnosis spoken aloud.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Stave 3, the extract — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 3, the Cratchit dinner — family inside poverty)

• Topic: At the novella's central scene of family, Dickens names collective joy generated inside material poverty by the patient organisation of small acts.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Mrs. Cratchit made the gravy (ready beforehand in a little saucepan) hissing hot; Master Peter mashed the potatoes with incredible vigour; Miss Belinda sweetened up the apple-sauce; Martha dusted the hot plates" combines polysyndeton-by-semicolon (each clause balanced and joined into a chain of communal action) with anaphora-by-naming (each sentence opening with the name of a different family member, so the family is built syntactically by being listed).

• Close analysis: The grammatical parallelism — name + verb + small object repeated four times — turns the listing itself into the picture of family; the dish on the table is built by every named hand.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader absorbs each family member contribute and feels the dinner being assembled in real time.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader picks up that the Cratchit goose, comically tiny, is enough — abundance is not material but relational.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be writing against the period's utilitarian arithmetic — the Cratchit table proves that the ledger's calculation of food per pauper has misnamed the unit of measurement; family is the unit, not the head.

• Context (AO3): The 1834 Poor Law had broken families on entry to the workhouse; the Cratchit dinner is the dramatised refusal of that breaking, the household visibly intact at the very point the law had said it could not be.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Stave 5, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 5, Fred's house — the family threshold finally crossed)

Will you let me in, Fred?" combines fragmented declarative simplicity (four short sentences in a row, each grammatically complete) with the imperative-softened-into-question ("Will you let me in" — a man asking permission at his own nephew's door).

• Close analysis: The short fragments imitate a man whose closed sentences can no longer hold their old weight; the question-form replaces the Stave-1 "Bah! Humbug!" as the new signature speech-act.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader infers the fragmented syntax and feels the vulnerability of the closed self at the moment its door begins to open.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader detects that the family door was always the readiest door for the closed self to cross — narrower than charity, closer than the workhouse, opened from the inside the moment the closed self knocks.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be writing isolation as a curable injury — the closed self can still cross a threshold, provided the threshold is a family one and the recognition has arrived in time.

• Context (AO3): Dickens's own near-singular role in turning Christmas into a family festival made the household door the central symbolic threshold of the new festival; Stave 5 dramatises that crossing as the practical end-point of redemption.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through the narrator's Stave-1 "solitary as an oyster" portrait, the Stave-2 boarding-school boy whose isolation hardened into the closed Stave-1 self, and the Stave-5 family door finally crossed, Dickens presents isolation as both injury and consequence — the cash-nexus self the closed boy was forced to harden into.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that money without mercy is a form of living death, and that isolation is the cash-nexus's first and last symptom — the closed self the cash-nexus produces and the closed self that, alone, produces nothing back.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be inverting the moral economy of his own opening Stave — Scrooge is unforgivable as character but explicable as biography, the closed self diagnosed as injury before the diagnosis becomes verdict.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that isolation is curable, that the cure is the smallest local threshold, and that the door from the "feeble fire" of the boarding-school to Fred's lit dining-room stays open until the gravestone closes it.

Q9 — The importance of the Christmas spirit (Fred)

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Dickens presents the importance of the Christmas spirit in A Christmas Carol.

>

Write about:

>

- how Dickens presents the Christmas spirit in this extract

- how Dickens presents the Christmas spirit in the novel as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Tricolon / triadic listing = three items in a row, often building emotional weight.

• Biblical allusion = reference back to the Bible ("fellow-passengers to the grave" — the equality of all souls before death).

• Compound adjective = adjective made by joining words ("shut-up").

• Polysyndeton = many conjunctions producing inclusive abundance.

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Fred as foil to Scrooge).

• Apostrophe = direct address (Fred's "God bless it!").

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Prince Albert's Christmas tree had arrived in England in 1841, and Henry Cole had commissioned the first commercial Christmas card in 1843 — by the time A Christmas Carol appeared that December, the festival was being culturally re-built faster than at any other moment in English history.

• Building sentence (historical context): That re-building sat against an older religious controversy — Sabbatarian campaigners had been pressing Parliament since the 1830s for stricter Sunday observance, and the Oxford Movement (1833 onward) was reshaping High-Church Anglican theology. Carlyle's Past and Present (April 1843) had argued that the cash-nexus had hollowed out every other moral relationship the festival might restore.

• Thesis: Across Fred's Stave-1 "fellow-passengers to the grave" manifesto, the Stave-3 Cratchit dinner where Christmas arrives at a poor table the cash-nexus had refused to feed, and the Stave-5 awakening that converts the closed self into a festival participant, Dickens presents the Christmas spirit not as private celebration but as the moral architecture of a national ritual the wealthy refuse at their cost.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Stave 1, the extract — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 1, Fred's manifesto — the festival defined)

• Topic: In Fred's manifesto, Dickens defines the Christmas spirit through its capacity to dissolve the social barriers the rest of the year keeps deliberately closed.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "a kind, forgiving, charitable, pleasant time… the only time I know of, in the long calendar of the year, when men and women seem by one consent to open their shut-up hearts freely" combines a tricolon-extended-into-quatrain ("kind, forgiving, charitable, pleasant", four ascending moral virtues) with the compound adjective "shut-up" (hearts as houses with closed doors and shutters drawn).

• Close analysis: The four-virtue list escalates from the practical "kind" through the religious "forgiving" to the social "charitable" and lands on the gentle "pleasant"; the compound "shut-up" makes the heart a building with closeable doors that the festival reopens.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader understands the rising rhythm of the four-virtue list and feels the festival defined as widening moral capacity.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader interprets the "shut-up hearts" as a diagnosis of the eleven months of the year outside Christmas — the festival does not invent kindness but reopens what the rest of the calendar has shut.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be writing through Fred for the readership he wants to convert — the festival is the moral architecture inside which the cash-nexus self can be unforged for one day, and the test is whether the reopened door stays open afterwards.

• Context (AO3): Dickens's own near-singular role in turning Christmas into a national festival made Fred's manifesto, in retrospect, one of the founding documents of the Victorian Christmas — the festival defined not by religious observance but by moral architecture.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Stave 3, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 3, the Cratchit dinner — Christmas at the poor table)

• Topic: At the novella's central scene of Christmas, Dickens names the festival arriving at a table the cash-nexus had refused to feed — moral abundance generated inside material poverty.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "and even Tiny Tim, excited by the two young Cratchits, beat on the table with the handle of his knife, and feebly cried Hurrah!" combines polysyndeton ("and… and… and…") with the antithesis between the verb "beat" (full force) and the adverb "feebly" (the disabled child's weakened reach).

• Close analysis: The polysyndetic "and" binds the action into a single rush of communal joy; the antithesis between "beat" and "feebly" names exactly the moral tenderness the festival enables — full effort from a body whose force is small.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader discerns the "feebly cried Hurrah!" and feels the disproportion between the smallness of the body and the size of the joy.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader grasps the festival operating exactly where the cash-nexus had said it could not — at a poor clerk's table, on fifteen shillings a week, with a goose comically too small.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be writing the Christmas spirit as the festival of disproportion — small bodies producing large joy, small geese feeding whole families, small wages stretched into a relational abundance the wealthy ledger had said was impossible.

• Context (AO3): The 1834 New Poor Law had institutionalised the doctrine that pauper-suffering was deserved and economic; the Cratchit dinner is the dramatised refusal of that doctrine, the festival lit on a table the workhouse-doctrine had said could not afford it.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Stave 5, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 5, Tim's blessing — the festival as ultimate moral)

• Topic: In the closing Stave, Dickens enacts the Christmas spirit as the controlling concept's final landing point — the smallest character speaking the smallest sentence in the festival's universal pronoun.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "And so, as Tiny Tim observed, God bless Us, Every One!" combines the universal pronoun "Us, Every One" (collective + individual held together) with the Beatitudes-register imperative "God bless" (a blessing-formula drawn from Matthew 5).

• Close analysis: The pronoun pairing — "Us" (the household, the festival, the city) and "Every One" (each person inside that "Us") — holds the collective and the individual at once; the "God bless" lands the message in the religious register the festival ought to carry.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader sees the smallest voice deliver the largest moral and feels the festival's central principle land in twelve words.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader observes the smallest character as the controlling concept's most authoritative speaker — the festival's authority pointedly relocated from the wealthy man's counting-house to the disabled child's chair.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be writing the festival's final moral in the mouth of its smallest member — the cash-nexus society had named the Cratchits and Tims as "surplus population"; the festival names them as "Every One" and lets them bless the rest.

• Context (AO3): The novella appeared at exactly the moment Dickens and his contemporaries were re-inventing the modern English Christmas — Prince Albert's tree (1841), Henry Cole's first commercial Christmas card (1843), and Dickens's own Sabbatarian-counter-argument that the festival's job was moral architecture, not religious enforcement.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through Fred's Stave-1 manifesto on the "shut-up hearts" the festival reopens, the Stave-3 Cratchit dinner where Christmas arrives at a table the ledger had refused to feed, and Tim's Stave-5 blessing on "Us, Every One", Dickens presents the Christmas spirit not as private celebration but as moral architecture — a national ritual the wealthy refuse at their cost.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that money without mercy is a form of living death, and that the festival is the architecture inside which charity made active becomes rebirth.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be writing one of the founding documents of the modern Christmas — the festival defined not by religious observance but by the smallest local act of opening the "shut-up hearts" the cash-nexus had been keeping closed.

• Ultimate moral: The final message, given to the smallest character in the smallest sentence, is that the festival's authority lives in "Every One", and that the test of the redeemed self is whether the festival has stayed open for the rest of the calendar.

Q10 — Social responsibility (the duty of the wealthy to the poor)

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Dickens presents ideas about social responsibility in A Christmas Carol.

>

Write about:

>

- how Dickens presents social responsibility in this extract

- how Dickens presents social responsibility in the novel as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Euphemism = polite language disguising something harsher ("surplus population" refusing to name the dying poor).

• Allegory = a narrative in which figures stand for abstract ideas.

• Imperative = grammatical command ("Beware!").

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Biblical register = vocabulary drawn from the Bible ("Doom", "Beware").

• Polysyndeton = many conjunctions producing inclusive abundance.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Malthus's 1798 Essay on the Principle of Population argued that excess paupers had to be deterred by suffering rather than relieved.

• Building sentence (historical context): The 1834 Poor Law Amendment Act had institutionalised that doctrine in the workhouse, abolishing outdoor relief for the able-bodied poor on the principle of "less eligibility". Carlyle's Past and Present (April 1843) had named the cash-nexus that bound rich to poor through ledgers alone, asking the question Dickens's novella exists to dramatise: to whom does the wealthy person owe what?

• Thesis: Across Scrooge's Stave-1 echo of the Malthusian doctrine in the charity-gentlemen exchange, the Stave-3 allegorical revelation of Ignorance and Want as the "Doom" the wealthy had refused to read, and the Stave-5 charity gentleman met in the street and the Cratchit raise that converts moral recognition into payment, Dickens presents social responsibility as the question the cash-nexus century had been refusing to answer — and as the question whose only adequate answer is local, immediate and practical.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Stave 1, the extract — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 1, the charity gentlemen — responsibility refused)

• Topic: In the charity-gentlemen exchange, Dickens reveals social responsibility refused — Scrooge speaking the wealthy class's quiet doctrine in its bluntest moral form.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "If they would rather die, they had better do it, and decrease the surplus population" combines euphemism (the abstract noun "surplus population" refusing to name the dying poor as people) with the conditional structure "If they would rather die, they had better" (a formulation that disguises a death-sentence as economic advice).

• Close analysis: The phrase "surplus population" is borrowed from Malthusian economics and erases human individuality at the level of vocabulary; the conditional pretends to defer to the dying person's preference while in fact removing the wealthy speaker's responsibility entirely.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader tracks the doctrine in its bluntest form and registers the casual cruelty of polite economic language.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader observes Scrooge speaking the wealthy class's quiet doctrine — the diagnosis the period had handed him, ready-made.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be exposing the wealth-class's doctrine by putting it in the mouth of his most caricatured villain — a strategy that allows the reader to recognise his or her own period speaking through Scrooge.

• Context (AO3): Malthus's 1798 Essay had supplied the period with its arithmetic for ignoring the poor; the 1834 New Poor Law had institutionalised it; Scrooge speaks the language of both.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Stave 3, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 3, Ignorance and Want — responsibility named)

• Topic: At the novella's most explicitly political moment, Dickens makes social responsibility unmissable as allegory — two children whose names are the abstract nouns the wealthy reader has been refusing to look at.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "This boy is Ignorance. This girl is Want. Beware them both, and all of their degree, but most of all beware this boy, for on his brow I see that written which is Doom" combines allegorical naming (the children as personified ideas) with the imperative "Beware" (the Spirit issuing direct moral command, repeated for emphasis).

• Close analysis: The naming-by-abstract-noun strips the figures of individuality so they stand for an entire class of suffering; the repeated imperative "Beware" converts the wealthy reader from observer into addressee, and the biblical-register "Doom" lifts the warning into Old-Testament authority.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader sees the children and recognises the abstract policy-language of "surplus population" has had specific bodies in it all along.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader experiences the imperative "Beware!" land directly and feels the moral weight transferred from Spirit to Scrooge to reader.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be using allegory — the medieval mode where moral meaning is named on the surface — to make social responsibility unmissable for a readership skilled at not seeing it.

• Context (AO3): Edwin Chadwick's 1842 Sanitary Report had documented child mortality in the rookeries Dickens had walked through earlier that autumn; "Doom" on Ignorance's brow is the policy-document warning rendered in Old-Testament register.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Stave 5, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Stave 5, the charity gentleman + the Cratchit raise — responsibility paid)

• Topic: In the closing Stave, Dickens dramatises social responsibility converted into local immediate payment — the Stave-1 doctrine answered not by argument but by money on a counter and a wage raised mid-sentence.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "Don't say anything, please. Come and see me. Will you come and see me?" combines fragmented declarative simplicity (Scrooge's speech broken into three short clauses, each grammatically complete) with a verbal shift from imperative ("Come and see me") to interrogative ("Will you come and see me?") inside the same utterance.

• Close analysis: The three short clauses refuse the wealthy class's polite vocabulary of munificence; the verbal shift from command to question relocates the relation from cheque to home and asks the receiver's permission for the visit.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader encounters the fragmented syntax and feels the relation re-personalised in real time.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader experiences that the answer to the Stave-1 "surplus population" doctrine is not philosophy but the smallest local invitation — "Come and see me".

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be writing for an employer-class readership that had grown comfortable with annual donations and abstract munificence; the cure for the cash-nexus is the personal threshold of the wealthy man's own door.

• Context (AO3): Carlyle's Past and Present had argued the wealthy classes needed conversion not policy; Stave 5's "Come and see me" is the conversion enacted at the smallest local scale, the wealthy door opened from the inside.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, through Scrooge's Stave-1 echo of the Malthusian "surplus population" doctrine, the Stave-3 allegorical revelation of Ignorance and Want, and the Stave-5 charity gentleman met in the street and the Cratchit raise, Dickens presents social responsibility as the question the cash-nexus century had been refusing to answer — and as a question whose only adequate answer is local, immediate and practical.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that money without mercy is a form of living death, and that the cure for the closed wealthy self is the smallest personal threshold opened from the inside.

• Author's purpose: Dickens may be writing not a policy-document but a moral primer — the test of a redeemed reader is whether the "surplus population" in front of him has become a household with a name.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the cash-nexus's first violence is the abstract noun, that the cure for the abstract noun is the personal invitation, and that the door from "surplus population" to "Come and see me" stays open until the gravestone closes it.

If a question arrives whose specific theme is not on this list, recombine — almost every AQA A Christmas Carol theme can be answered with a Stave 1 + a Stave 3 + a Stave 5 trio drawn from the rows above, plus one of the two critical voices below woven into a body-paragraph close.

Three context factors that travel across all 10 questions

These three context factors are the period-anchors most useful inside any Stave. Pick one per body paragraph, thread it briefly into the close analysis, and never bolt it on as a stand-alone sentence at the end of the essay.

1. The Hungry Forties + the 1834 New Poor Law — Most useful for: Q4, Q5, Q9, Q10, and any question on family / poverty / social responsibility / the festival arriving at a poor table. Dickens's Field Lane Ragged School visit (September 1843) and Manchester Athenaeum address (5 October 1843) are the autobiographical hooks. Edwin Chadwick's Sanitary Report (1842) and the Andover Workhouse scandal (1845) are the surrounding policy detail. The 1834 Act's "less eligibility" principle and the segregation of pauper families on entry to the workhouse are the two key legal hooks.

2. Carlyle's Past and Present (April 1843) — Most useful for: Q1, Q2, Q6, Q7, Q8 — any question on Scrooge / Marley / employer-relations / the closed self / the supernatural-as-moral-pedagogy. Carlyle coined "the cash-nexus" eight months before the novella appeared and supplied Dickens with the diagnostic vocabulary; Carlyle's idealisation of Abbot Samson as a model "captain of industry" is the model behind Fezziwig.

3. The invented Christmas (Prince Albert's tree, 1841; Henry Cole's first commercial Christmas card, 1843; Dickens's own near-singular role) — Most useful for: Q3, Q4, Q7, Q9 — any question on redemption / family / generosity / the Christmas spirit. The festival was being culturally rebuilt at exactly the moment Dickens was writing, and the novella is one of the founding documents of that rebuilding; Stave 5 is also the festival as Dickens wished it to operate the rest of the year.

Two critical voices that travel across all 10 questions

Pick one critical voice per essay. Name the critic + their work + their year. Embed the critical voice inside a body-paragraph close, NOT as a stand-alone sentence in the conclusion.

1. George Orwell, "Charles Dickens" (1940, in Inside the Whale) — Orwell argues that Dickens is a moral revolutionary rather than a political one — "His radicalism is of the vaguest kind, and yet one always knows that it is there. … His whole 'message' is one that at first glance looks like an enormous platitude: If men would behave decently the world would be decent." Apply to: any question about redemption / the closed self / employer relations / social responsibility — Orwell's reading frames the novella as moral conversion rather than policy.

2. Robert Douglas-Fairhurst, The Story of A Christmas Carol (Vintage, 2017) — Douglas-Fairhurst documents Dickens's near-singular role in inventing the modern Christmas, reading the novella as moral architecture for a festival being rebuilt in real time. Apply to: any question about Christmas / family / generosity / the festival as moral ritual — Douglas-Fairhurst's reading frames Stave 5 as one of the founding documents of the Victorian festival.

Reminder

• One context factor per body paragraph, threaded into the close, never bolted on at the end.

• One critical voice per essay, named with year, embedded inside a body-paragraph close.

• The controlling concept's three claims — money without mercy is living death; charity made active is rebirth; the door stays open until the gravestone closes it — should land at least once across the introduction's thesis and the conclusion's restated thesis.

• The locked structure — Intro 3 / BP1-2-3 each 7 / Conclusion 4 — is non-negotiable. AQA examiners read for shape; the page must look like an essay.

Total target: ~1000 words on the page in the exam. Always under 1100. Always close with the smallest-character-largest-moral logic of "God bless Us, Every One!" — the controlling concept's final landing point.
